summary: Animates a single still into a short clip. The image carries the composition; the prompt describes the motion. This is the capsule to reach for when you already have a frame you like.

inputs.name: image
inputs.description: Single still. The first frame is conditioned on this. Read locally and never transmitted.

inputs.name: prompt
inputs.description: Describe the motion, not the still. The image already carries composition, subject and lighting; restating them wastes conditioning.

inputs.name: negative_prompt
inputs.description: Motion artifacts to suppress. Leave empty unless you are fixing a specific observed failure.

inputs.name: seed
inputs.description: Omit for a random seed. The drawn value is recorded in the sidecar either way, so any run can be repeated exactly.

inputs.name: duration_s
inputs.description: Clip length in seconds. Frame count is derived from this and fps.

outputs.name: video
outputs.description: H.264, yuv420p, so it plays everywhere without transcoding.

outputs.name: sidecar
outputs.description: Model id, weight hash, resolved profile id, drawn seed, and every resolved setting. Enough to reproduce this run exactly.

preflight.message: No execution profile matches this GPU for the wan family or its fallbacks. Run `veizik doctor` to see the support tier for each family on this machine.
Free VRAM is below the resolved profile's expected peak plus a 1 GiB working margin. Close other GPU work, or pick a capsule with a lower resolution.
Less than 2 GiB free on the output volume. The clip and its intermediates will not fit.
The input image could not be decoded, or its pixel format is unsupported. Checked before any weights are loaded so the failure costs seconds rather than minutes.
The input image is smaller than 256 px on its short edge. It will be upscaled to the render resolution, which usually reads as soft.

criteria.note: A non-finite value in the output latents means the render is garbage. This is the one criterion worth failing the run over, because there is nothing for the operator to judge.
More than 10 percent fully black frames is the classic i2v collapse. Flagged, not failed — a deliberate fade to black is a legitimate result.
A near-frozen clip usually means the motion prompt was ignored. Try a more explicit verb before raising the step count.
Guards against a truncated write. A mismatch here points at the encoder rather than the model.

status_note: The definition is complete and stable, and the parameters below are what the runtime will use. Execution is NOT shipped: the native engine path for this capsule is not in the public download, and the public experimental backend covers universal t2v/t2i only (Linux + NVIDIA, with a torch/diffusers environment you supply). Read this file as a contract, not as a feature you can run today.
notes: Prompt the motion, not the picture. The two most common failure modes are a static clip (verb too vague) and drift away from the input identity late in the clip (shorten duration_s before raising steps). No timing figure appears in this file, because none has been measured under the published benchmark method and reproduced.
